subject: Mathematics
q: An arc \[\overset{\huge\frown}{PQ}\] subtends an angle of \[60^\circ\] at the centre of a circle of radius \[2\] cm. What is the length of arc \[PQ\]?
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback: \begin{align}r&= 2\\\theta= 60^\circ&=\frac{\pi}{3} \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Length of a circular arc, } l&=r \theta\\\\l&= 2 \times \frac{\pi}{3}\\\\l&= \frac{2\pi}{3}\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the exact distance travelled by a point moving on a circle of radius \[44\] m if the point completes \[2.2\] revolutions.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback:
Recall that \[1\] complete revolution of a circle is equal to \[2π\].

\begin{align}l&=rθ\\θ &= 2.2 \times 2 π\\l&=44\times(2.2×2π)\\&=193.6π\end{align}

Therefore, for \[2.2\] revolutions by a point moving on this circle, the distance travelled will be \[193.6π\] m.

subject: Mathematics
q: A pendulum that is \[14\] cm long oscillates through an angle of \[18^\circ\]. Find the length of the path described by its extremity.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback:
From \[l=rθ\], we obtain

\begin{align}l&=14×18^\circ\\\\&=14×\frac{18}{180}×π\\\\&=14×\frac{1}{10}×π\\\\&=\frac{7π}{5}\end{align}

Hence, the length of the path is \[\frac{7π}{5}\] cm.

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the radius of the sector shown below, where the arc length is \[16\] cm.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback:
From \[l=rθ\], we obtain

\begin{align}r&=\frac{l}{θ}\\\\l&= 16 \mbox{ cm}\\\\θ &= \frac{5π}{4}\\\\&=\frac{16}{\frac{5π}{4}}\\\\&= \frac{16 \times 4}{5 π}\\\\& ≈4.08\end{align}

Therefore, the radius of this sector is \[4.08\] cm.

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the area of the sector shown below.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback: \begin{align}θ&=60^\circ\\\\&=60×\frac{π}{180}\\\\&=1.0472 \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Area of a sector} &=\frac{1}{2}r^2 θ\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}&=\frac{1}{2}(3)^2 (1.0472)\\\\&= \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 1.0472\\\\&=4.5 \times 1.0472\\\\&≈4.71 \mbox{ m}^2\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the area of a sector with \[θ= 9^\circ 25'\] and \[r= 4\] m.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback:
Recall that \[1\] degree equals \[60\] minutes, and \[1\] minute is equal to \[60\] seconds.

π radians \[= 180^\circ\].

\begin{align}9^\circ 25'&=\left ( 9+\frac{25}{60} \right )^\circ\times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right) \mbox{ radians}\\\\9^\circ 25'&=9.4167 \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right ) \mbox{ radians}\\\\9^\circ 25'&=0.1644 \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Area of a sector} &=\frac{1}{2}r^2 θ\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}& =\frac{1}{2}(4)^2 (0.1644)\\\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times 16 \times 0.1644\\\\&=8 \times 0.1644\\\\& ≈1.31\end{align}

Therefore, the area of a sector with \[θ= 9^\circ 25'\] and \[r= 4\] m is \[1.31\mbox{ m}^2\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the area of a sector with \[θ=40^\circ 30'\] and \[r= 15\] m.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback:
Recall that \[1\] degree equals \[60\] minutes, \[1\] minute is equal to \[60\] seconds, and \[π\] radians \[= 180^\circ\].

\begin{align}40^\circ 30'&=\left ( 40+\frac{30}{60} \right )^\circ \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right )\mbox{ radians}\\\\&=40.5 \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right )\mbox{ radians}\\\\&=0.7069 \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Area of a sector}&=\frac{1}{2}r^2 θ\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}&=\frac{1}{2} (15)^2 (0.7069)\\\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times 225 \times 0.7069\\\\&= 112.5 \times 0.7069\\\\& ≈79.5\end{align}

Therefore, the area of a sector with \[θ=40^\circ 30'\] and \[r= 15\] m is \[79.5\mbox{ m}^2\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the area of a sector with \[θ= 25^\circ 50' 40''\] and \[r= 8\] m.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback:
Recall that \[1\] degree equals \[60\] minutes, \[1\] minute is equal to \[60\] seconds, and \[π\] radians \[= 180^\circ\].

\begin{align}25^\circ 50' 40''&=\left ( 25+\frac{50}{60}+\frac{40}{3600} \right )^\circ \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right )\mbox{ radians}\\\\&=(25+0.83333+0.0111) \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right )\mbox{ radians}\\\\&=25.8444 \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right ) \mbox{ radians}\\\\&=0.4508 \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Area of a sector}&=\frac{1}{2}r^2 θ\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}&=\frac{1}{2} (8)^2 (0.4508)\\\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times 64 \times 0.4508\\\\&= 32 \times 0.4508\\\\& ≈ 14.4\end{align}

Therefore, the area of a sector with \[θ= 25^\circ 50' 40''\] and \[r= 8\] m is \[14.4\mbox{ m}^2\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the area of a sector with \[θ=65^\circ 40' 50''\] and \[r= 11\] m.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback:
Recall that \[1\] degree equals \[60\] minutes, \[1\] minute is equal to \[60\] seconds, and \[π\] radians \[= 180^\circ\].

\begin{align}25^\circ 50' 40''&=\left ( 65+\frac{40}{60}+\frac{50}{3600} \right )^\circ \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right )\mbox{ radians}\\\\&=(65+0.6666+0.01388) \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right )\mbox{ radians}\\\\&=65.6805 \times \left ( \frac{π}{180} \right ) \mbox{ radians}\\\\&=1.1462 \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Area of a sector}&=\frac{1}{2}r^2 θ\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}&=\frac{1}{2} (11)^2 (1.1462)\\\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times 121 \times 1.1462\\\\&= 60.5 \times 1.1462\\\\& ≈ 69.3\end{align}

Therefore, the area of a sector with \[θ=65^° 40' 50''\] and \[r= 11\] m is \[69.3 \mbox{ m}^2\].

subject: Mathematics
q:
A closed hemisphere needs to be covered in paint on all sides. What measure of area needs to be painted?
\[(\pi = \frac{22}{7})\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a sphere & solve related real life problems
feedback:
To find out the total area of the hemisphere that needs to be painted, we must calculate its total surface area.

The surface area of the hemisphere is made up of a curved surface and a circular top. The curved surface area will be calculated by the formula \[4\pi r^2\div 2\], since it is a hemisphere, and the circular top will be calculated by the formula \[\pi r^2\]. The radius of the sphere is given as \[28\] cm.

\begin{align} \mbox{Total Surface Area} &= \frac {4 \pi r^2}{2} + \pi r^2\\ SA&= \left[4 \times \frac {22}{7} \times (28)^2\right]\div 2 + \left[\frac{22}{7} \times (28)^2\right]\\\\ &=\left[\frac{88 \times 784}{7} \right]\div 2 + \left[\frac {22}{7} \times 784\right] \\\\ &=\left[\frac{68992}{7}\right]\div 2 + \left[\frac{17248}{7}\right]\\\\ &=\frac{9856}{2} + 2464\\\\ &=4928 + 2464\\\\ &=7392\\\\ \therefore \mbox{Total Surface Area} = 7392 \ \mbox{cm}^2\\ \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
Find the surface area of a sphere, rounded to the nearest tenth, given its diameter is \[19\] metres.
\[(\pi = \frac{22}{7})\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a sphere & solve related real life problems
feedback:
If the diameter of a sphere is \[19\] metres, its radius is \[19\div2 = 9.5\] metres.
Using this value in the formula to calculate surface area, we get:

\begin{align} \\SA&=4 \pi r^2\\ SA&= 4 \times \frac {22}{7} \times (9.5)^2\\\\ &=\frac{88}{7} \times 90.25 \\\\ &=\frac{7942}{7}\\\\ &=1134.57\\ \end{align}

Rounding this value to the nearest tenth, or one decimal place:

\begin{align} SA &= 1134.6 \ \mbox{cm}^2\\ \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Sara is going to fill this serving bowl with soup. If the serving bowl is an exact hemisphere, can you calculate how many litres of soup Sara must cook to fill the bowl to the top? (\[1\] litre \[=1000\] cm\[^3\])
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a sphere & solve related real life problems
feedback:
To find out the amount of soup Sara must cook to fill this bowl, we will have to calculate its volume. We can see that the radius of the hemisphere is \[21\] cm.

Since the formula to calculate the volume of a sphere is \[V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\], the volume of a hemisphere will be half the volume of a full sphere. This will be calculated as:

\begin{align} V&=\left[ \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \right] \div{2}\\\\ V&=\left[ \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 21^{3} \right] \div{2}\\\\ &=\left[ \frac{88 \times 21^3}{3 \times 7} \right] \div{2}\\\\ &=\left[ \frac{88 \times 21^2 \times 21}{21} \right] \div{2}\\\\ &= \left[ 88 \times 21^2 \right] \div{2}\\\\ &=\left[88 \times 441 \right] \div{2}\\\\ &= \left[38808\right] \div{2}\\\\ &=19404\\\\ \therefore \mbox{Volume of hemisphere} = V&= 19404\ \mbox{cm}^{3}\\ \end{align}

Converting volume from cubic centimetres to litres:

\begin{align} \mbox{Since} \ 1 \ \mbox{litre} &= 1000 \ \mbox{cm}^3\\\\ \therefore 19404\ \mbox{cm}^3 &= \frac {19404}{1000}\ \mbox{litres} \\\\ \mbox{Volume in litres} &= 19.404 \ \mbox{liters}\\ \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
A hemisphere is half a sphere. The solid hemisphere shown in the figure has a radius \[\overline{OB}\] of \[42\] cm. Can you find the amount of material used to make this hemisphere?
\[(\pi = \frac{22}{7})\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a sphere & solve related real life problems
feedback:
To find out the amount of material used to make this hemisphere, we will have to calculate its volume. Since the formula to calculate the volume of a sphere is \[V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\], the volume of a hemisphere will be half the volume of a full sphere and calculated as:

\begin{align} V&=\left[ \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \right] \div{2}\\\\ V&=\left[ \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 42^{3} \right] \div{2}\\\\ &=\left[ \frac{88 \times 42^3}{3 \times 7} \right] \div{2}\\\\ &=\left[ \frac{88 \times 42^2 \times 6}{3} \right] \div{2}\\\\ &= \left[ 88 \times 42^2 \times 2 \right] \div{2}\\\\ &=\left[88 \times 1764 \times 2\right] \div{2}\\\\ &= \left[310464\right] \div{2}\\\\ &=155232\\\\ \therefore \mbox{Volume of hemisphere} = V&= 155232\ \mbox{cm}^{3}\\ \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
The surface area of a sphere is \[484 \pi\] cm\[^2\]. What is its volume, in terms of \[\pi\]?
\[(\pi = \frac{22}{7})\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a sphere & solve related real life problems
feedback:
We know that the formula to calculate surface area is \[4 \pi r^2\]. Since we know that the surface area is equal to \[484 \pi\] cm\[^2\], we can rewrite the following equation:

\begin{align} \\SA&=4 \pi r^2\\ \\484\pi &= 4 \pi \times r^2\\\\ \frac{484 \pi}{4 \pi}&= r^2\\\\ r^2 &= 121\\ \end{align}

Taking the square root of both sides:

\begin{align} r &= \sqrt{121}\\ r&= 11\\ \therefore \mbox{radius} = r =11 \ \mbox{cm}\\ \end{align}

We can use the value of the radius to find the volume of the sphere:

\begin{align} V&=\frac{4}{3} \pi \times r^{3}\\\\ &=\frac{4 \pi}{3} \times 11^3\\\\ &=\frac{4 \pi}{3} \times 1331\\\\ &=\frac{5324 \pi}{3}\\\\ &=1774.66 \pi\\\\ \therefore V&= 1774.66\pi \ \mbox{cm}^{3}\\ \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
The volume of an ice cream cone is \[60\] cm\[^{3}\] and its radius is \[6\] cm. Find its height.
\[\left(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
We can use the volume of the cone to find out its height. By substituting the values in the formula:

\begin{align} \mbox{Volume of a cone}&=\frac{1}{3}\times\pi\times r^{2}\times h\\\\ 60&= \frac{1}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times 6^{2}\times h\\\\ 60&=\frac{22}{7}\times12\times h\\\\ 60&=\frac{264}{7}\times h\\\\ 420&= 264\times h\\\\ h &= \frac{420}{264} \\\\ h&=1.59 \ \mbox{cm} \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the area of the curved surface of right circular cone is \[55\] cm\[^{2}\], and its slant height is \[7\] cm, find the area of the circular base.
\[\left(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
To find the area of the circular base of a cone, we can use the formula. However, first we need to find the radius of the base. We can use the area of the curved surface to find the radius:

\begin{align} \mbox{Area of curved surface}&=\pi\times r\times l\\ 55&=\frac{22}{7}\times r\times7\\\\ 55&= 22\times r\\ r&= \frac{55}{22}\\\\ r&=2.5 \ \mbox{cm} \end{align}

Now that we know the radius of the cone, we can use the formula to find the area of the circular base:

\begin{align} \mbox{Area of circular base }&=\pi\times r^{2}\\ &=\frac{22}{7}\times 2.5^{2}\\\\ &=\frac{22}{7}\times 6.25\\\\ &=\frac{137.5}{7}\\\\ \mbox{Area of circular base }&=19.64 \mbox{ cm}^2 \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
Water is flowing out of a conical funnel at \[50\] liters per minute. If the radius of the funnel is \[2.5\] m and its height is \[6\] m, how long will it take for a full funnel of water to drain? Remember \[1\] m\[^{3}\] is equal to \[1{,}000\] liters.
\[\left(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
Let's begin by finding the volume of the conical funnel:

\begin{align} V&=\frac{1}{3}\times\pi\times r^{2}\times h\\\\ &=\frac{1}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times 2.5^{2}\times6\\\\ &=\frac{22}{7}\times 6.25\times2\\\\ &=\frac{275}{7}\\\\ &=39.286 \end{align}

\[\therefore\] Volume of water in the tank \[= 39.286\] m\[^3\]

Converting volume from cubic metres to liters:

\begin{align} \mbox{Since } 1{,}000 \mbox{ liters} &= 1 \mbox{ m}^3\\\\ \therefore 39.286 \mbox{ m}^3 &= 39.286 \times 1{,}000 \mbox{ liters} \\\\ \mbox{Volume in liters} &= 39{,}286 \mbox{ liters} \end{align}

The time taken for the funnel to empty at a rate of \[50\] liters per minute can be calculated as:

\begin{align} \mbox{Time taken to empty } 50 \mbox{ liters of water} &= 1 \mbox{ minute}\\ \mbox{Time taken to empty } 1 \mbox{ liter of water}& = \frac{1}{50} \mbox{ minutes}\\\\ \therefore \mbox{Time taken to empty } 39{,}286 \mbox{ liters of water}& = \frac{1}{50} \times 39{,}286 \mbox{ minutes}\\\\ &= 785.72 \mbox{ minutes}\\\\ & = 13 \mbox{ hours} \ 6 \mbox{ minutes} \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following statements about the right circular cone is NOT true?
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
For a right circular cone, you can draw a straight line from the tip of the cone to the centre of the circular base. At \[O\], you can make a right angle at \[90\] degrees. Therefore, you can use the Pythagoras theorem to find the radius, height or slant height of the cone:

\begin{align} \mbox{Pythagoras theorem: } \left(\mbox{hypotenuse}\right)^{2}&= \left(\mbox{base}\right)^{2} + \left(\mbox{perpendicular}\right)^{2}\\ \left(\mbox{slant height}\right)^{2}&= \left(\mbox{radius}\right)^{2} + \left(\mbox{height}\right)^{2}\\ m\overline{BC}^{2} &= m\overline{OC}^{2} + m\overline{OB}^{2}\\ \mbox{Rearranging,}\\ m\overline{OC}^{2} &= m\overline{BC}^{2} - m\overline{OB}^{2}\\ \mbox{Rearranging,}\\ m\overline{BC}^{2} &= \sqrt{m\overline{OB}^{2}} + \sqrt{m\overline{OC}^{2}} \end{align}

Therefore, the incorrect option is \[m\overline{OB}^{2} = m\overline{BC}^{2} + m\overline{OC}^{2}\]

subject: Mathematics
q:
Fahad wants to fill his conical jar with sand. The jar has a diameter of \[10\] cm and has a slant height of \[13\] cm. How much sand can the jar contain?
\[\left(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
To find out how much sand the jar can contain, we need to calculate its volume. However, to find the volume, first we will need to calculate the height of the jar. If we divide the diameter by \[2\], we get the radius, which is \[5\] cm. Using Pythagoras theorem, we can calculate the height of the jar:

\begin{align} \mbox{Pythagoras theorem: } \left(\mbox{hypotenuse}\right)^{2}&= \left(\mbox{base}\right)^{2} + \left(\mbox{perpendicular}\right)^{2}\\ \left(\mbox{slant height}\right)^{2}&= \left(\mbox{radius}\right)^{2} + \left(\mbox{height}\right)^{2}\\ \left(\mbox{height}\right)^{2}&= \left(\mbox{slant height}\right)^{2} - \left(\mbox{radius}\right)^{2}\\ \left(\mbox{height}\right)^{2}&= \left(13\right)^{2} - \left(5\right)^{2}\\ h^{2}&=169-25\\ \sqrt{{h}^{2}}&=\sqrt{144}\\ h&=12\mbox{ cm} \end{align}

Now that we know the height of the cone, we can substitute the values in the formula:

\begin{align} \mbox{Volume of a cone }&=\frac{1}{3}\times\pi\times r^{2}\times h\\\\ &= \frac{1}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times 5^{2}\times12\\\\ &=\frac{22}{7}\times 5^{2}\times4\\\\ &=\frac{2{,}200}{7}\\\\ \mbox{Volume of the cone }&=314.29 \mbox{ cm}^3 \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
The volume of a right circular cone is \[190.93\] cm\[^{3}\] and its height is \[9\] cm. Find its radius.
\[\left (\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
We can use the volume of the cone to find its radius by substituting the values in the formula:

\begin{align} \mbox{Volume of a cone}&=\frac{1}{3}\times\pi\times r^{2}\times h\\\\ 190.93&= \frac{1}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times r^{2}\times9\\\\ 190.93&= \frac{66}{7}\times r^{2}\\\\ 1{,}336.51&= 66\times r^{2}\\\\ \frac{1{,}336.51}{66}&= r^{2}\\\\ \sqrt{20.25}&= \sqrt{r^{2}}\\ r&=4.5 \mbox{ cm} \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
A traffic cone is \[15\] cm tall and has a slant height of \[18\] cm. If the radius of the base is \[10\] cm, find the surface area and volume of the cone.
\[\left(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
We can use formulas to calculate the surface area and volume of the cone. For the surface area:

\begin{align} \mbox{Surface area of a cone }&=\pi\times r\times l + \pi\times r^{2}\\ &=\left(\frac{22}{7}\times10\times18\right) + \left(\frac{22}{7}\times 10^{2}\right)\\\\ &=\left(\frac{3{,}960}{7}\right) + \left(\frac{2{,}200}{7}\right)\\\\ &=\left(\frac{6{,}160}{7}\right)\\\\ \mbox{Surface area of a cone }&=880 \mbox{ cm}^2 \end{align}

For the volume of a cone:

\begin{align} \mbox{Volume of a cone }&=\frac{1}{3}\times\pi\times r^{2}\times h\\\\ &=\frac{1}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times 10^{2}\times 15\\\\ &=\frac{1}{3}\times\frac{33{,}000}{7}\\\\ &=\frac{33{,}000}{21}\\\\ \mbox{Volume of the cone }&=1{,}571.43 \ \mbox{cm}^3 \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
The area of the base of a right circular cone is \[28.26\] cm\[^2\]. If the height of the cone is \[4\] cm and the slant height is \[5\] cm, find its volume.
\[\left(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
To find the volume of a cone, first we need to calculate its radius. Since we know the area of the circular base, we can use this information to find the radius of the cone:

\begin{align} \mbox{Area of the circular base }&=\pi\times r^{2}\\\\ 28.26&=\frac{22}{7}\times r^{2}\\\\ 197.82&=22\times r^{2}\\\\ \frac{197.82}{22}&=r^{2}\\\\ \sqrt{8.99}&=\sqrt{r^{2}}\\ r&=3\mbox{ cm} \mbox{ or } 2.998 \mbox{ cm} \end{align}

Now that we know the radius, we can insert the values in the formula and calculate the volume of the cone:

\begin{align} \mbox{Volume of a cone }&=\frac{1}{3}\times\pi\times r^{2}\times h\\\\ &=\frac{1}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times 3^{2}\times 4\\\\ &=\frac{1}{3}\times\frac{792}{7}\\\\ &=\frac{792}{21}\\\\ \mbox{Volume of the cone }&=37.71 \mbox{ cm}^3 \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
The curved surface of a right circular cone has an area of \[264\] cm\[^2\]. The cone has a radius of \[12\] cm. Find its slant height.
\[\left(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
To find the slant height, we need to substitute the values in the formula for the curved surface of a cone:

\begin{align} \mbox{Area of curved surface }&=\pi\times r\times l\\ 264&=\frac{22}{7}\times12\times l\\\\ 264&= \frac{264}{7}\times l\\\\ 1{,}848&= {264}\times l\\\\ \frac{1{,}848}{264}&=l\\\\ l&=7 \mbox{ cm} \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
A metal sheet covers the curved surface as well as the circular base of a conical oil container. Its radius is \[10\] cm and the height of the container is \[24\] cm. What is the area of sheet used to make the container?



\[\left (\pi = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
The metal sheet will cover the oil container's curved surface as well as its circular base. Therefore, we need to calculate the total surface area of the container. As the slant height is unknown, first we have to use Pythagoras theorem to calculate the slant height:



\begin{align} \mbox{Pythagoras theorem: } \left(\mbox{hypotenuse}\right)^{2}&= \left(\mbox{base}\right)^{2} + \left(\mbox{perpendicular}\right)^{2}\\ \left(\mbox{slant height}\right)^{2}&= \left(\mbox{radius}\right)^{2} + \left(\mbox{height}\right)^{2}\\ \left(\mbox{slant height}\right)^{2}&= \left(10\right)^{2} + \left(24\right)^{2}\\ \sqrt{{l}^{2}}&=\sqrt{676}\\ l&=26\mbox{ cm} \end{align}

Now that we know the slant height, we can substitute the given values in the surface area formula:

\begin{align} \mbox{Surface area of a cone }&=\pi\times r\times l + \pi\times r^{2}\\\\ &=\left(\frac{22}{7}\times10\times26\right) + \left(\frac{22}{7}\times 10^{2}\right)\\\\ &=\left(\frac{5{,}720}{7}\right) + \left(\frac{2{,}200}{7}\right)\\\\ &=\left(\frac{7{,}920}{7}\right)\\\\ \mbox{Surface area of a cone }&=1{,}131.43\mbox{ cm}^2 \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
A party hat has a conical shape. It has a radius of \[8.4\] cm and its slant height is \[30\] cm. Find the area of the cardboard sheet used to prepare the hat.
\[\left (\pi\ = \frac{22}{7}\right)\]
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
To prepare the hat, the cardboard sheet needs to cover the curved surface of the hat. Therefore, we will calculate the area of the curved surface using the formula:

\begin{align} \mbox{Area of curved surface }&=\pi\times r\times l\\\\ &=\frac{22}{7}\times8.4\times30\\\\ &=\frac{5{,}544}{7}\\\\ \mbox{Area of curved surface }&=792 \mbox{ cm}^2 \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Ali has to make a conical tent. If the radius of the tent is denoted by \[r\], the slant height by \[l\], and the height by \[h\], which of the following formulas can we use to find out the amount of material he needs to make the tent?
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback: To make a conical tent, Ali will need enough material to cover its curved surface as well as its circular base. Therefore, he needs to calculate its total surface area. Surface area of a right circular cone is \[\pi\times r\times l+\pi\times r^{2}\].

subject: Mathematics
q: In the figure, the line \[\overline{BC}\] is known as the _____.
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback: The line connecting the vertex to any point on the circumference of the base is called the slant height. In this figure, \[\overline{OB}\] is the height, \[\overline{OC}\] is the radius and \[\overline{BC}\] is the slant height.

subject: Mathematics
q: The tip of a cone is called the _____.
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback:
The tip of the cone is called its vertex.

The length connecting the vertex to the centre of the base is called the height of the cone, while the distance from the top, along a side of the cone, to a point on the circumference of the base is called it's slant height.

subject: Mathematics
q: A cone is a solid, three-dimensional object that has a curved side and
skill: Find the surface area and volume of a cone and solve related real life problems
feedback: A cone is a solid, three-dimensional object that has a circular base, joined to a point by a curved side. For a cone, you can draw a straight line from the tip of the cone to the centre of the circular base. For a right circular cone, this line is perpendicular to the base.

q: In the figure below, the red line segment is a _____.
skill: Describe a circle and its components
feedback:
A chord is a straight line segment that joins any two points on the circle and a chord that goes through the centre of the circle is called a diameter.

Therefore, in the given figure, the red line segment is the diameter.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the figure below, the red line segment is a _____.
skill: Describe a circle and its components
feedback: A chord is a straight line segment that joins any two points on the circle. Thus, in the given figure, the red line segment is a chord.

skill: Describe a circle and its components
feedback:
A chord is a straight line segment that joins any two points on the circle and a chord that goes through the centre of the circle is called a diameter.

Thus, in the given figure, \[\overline{AB}\] is a chord, whereas \[\overline{CD}\] is a diameter.

\[\overline{OA}\], \[\overline{OC}\], and \[\overline{OD}\] are radial segments of the circle.

\[\widehat{AB}\] is a minor arc, whereas \[\widehat{ACB}\] is a major arc.

skill: Describe a circle and its components

\[\overline{OA}\], \[\overline{OC}\], and \[\overline{OD}\] are all radial segments of the circle.

\[\overline{AB}\] is a chord, whereas \[\overline{CD}\] is a diameter.

\[\widehat{AB}\] is a minor arc, whereas \[\widehat{ACB}\] is a major arc.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the following figure, what is the curve highlighted in blue called?
skill: Describe a circle and its components
feedback:
The circumference is the distance around the circle. In other words, the length of the outer boundary of the circle is its circumference.

A part of the circumference is called an arc. The arc could be of any length.

The blue arc is smaller than the red arc and is called the minor arc, whereas the red arc, or bigger arc, is called the major arc.

Thus, the curve, highlighted in blue, is called a minor arc.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the length of the line segment \[\overline{OC}\] is equal to \[8\] cm, then what is the length of the line segment \[\overline{BD}\]?
skill: Describe a circle and its components
feedback:
The line segment \[\overline{OC}\] is a radial segment and \[\overline{BD}\] is the diameter. The length of a diameter is equal to twice the radius. Thus,

\begin{align} d&=2r \\ &= 2 \times 8 \\ &= 16 \mbox{ cm} \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: If the length of the line segment \[\overline{OP}\] is equal to \[5\] cm, then what is the length of the line segment \[\overline{OQ}\]?
skill: Describe a circle and its components
feedback: The line segments \[\overline{OP}\] and \[\overline{OQ}\] are the radial segments and all radial segments of a circle are equal in length. Thus, \[m\overline{OP} = m\overline{OQ} = 5\] cm.

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following is a part of a circle?
skill: Describe a circle and its components
feedback: The centre, radius, chord, and diameter are not parts of a circle, but an arc is a part of a circle.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the following figure, line A is a _____, and line B is the _____.
skill: Concepts related to circles
feedback:
In the figure, line A is a chord, and line B is the radius.

A chord is a straight line segment which has its end points on the circumference of the circle and passes through the circle.

Recall that the radius of a circle is a line from the centre of the circle to its boundary or circumference.
The diameter of a circle is a line that goes from one end of a circle to the other and passes through the centre.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the given figure, what is the region highlighted in red called?
skill: Concepts related to circles
feedback: The region highlighted in red is called a segment. By definition, a region bounded by a chord and an arc is a segment.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the given figure, what is the curve highlighted in black called?
skill: Concepts related to circles
feedback: The curved line around a part of a circle is called an arc. It is any part of the boundary of the circle. If an arc is extended to draw the full circle, it becomes the circumference of the circle.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the given figure, the part highlighted in red is called a _____.
skill: Concepts related to circles
feedback: By definition, a region in a circle bounded by \[2\] radii and an arc is called a sector. Therefore, the highlighted part in the given figure is a sector. This can be understood from the following figure:

subject: Mathematics
q: In the given figure, where does point C lie?
options: in the exterior of the circle
in the interior of the circle
in the centre of the circle
skill: Concepts related to circles
feedback:
Point C is lying right on the boundary of the circle, therefore, it is neither an interior point nor an exterior point.
Note that points lying on the circumference of a circle are said to be lying ON the circle. Hence, point C is lying on the circle.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the given figure, point A is lying in the _____ of the circle.
skill: Concepts related to circles
feedback:
Any points lying outside the circle are said to be in the exterior of the circle. In this figure, point A is seen outside the circle, so it is lying in the exterior of the circle.

Point B is in the interior of the circle, while point C is said to be on the circle.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the given figure, point B is lying in the _____ of the circle.
skill: Concepts related to circles
feedback:
In the given circle, we can see that point B is lying inside the circle. Any points inside the circle are said to be lying in the interior of the circle.

Note that point B is not in the centre of the circle.

For this particular example, the two given sides are of measure \[6\: cm\] and \[8\: cm\] and the included angle between them is of measure \[45^{\circ}\].

First, we draw a line segment \[m\overline{BC} =8\: cm\]. Next, at point \[B\], we draw \[m∠DBC=45^{\circ}\] using a compass.

Then, with \[B\] as centre, we draw an arc of radius \[6\: cm \] to cut \[\overrightarrow{BD}\] at \[A\].

Finally, we join\[ A\] to \[C\] to form the required triangle.

q:
The steps to construct a triangle when two sides and the included angle are given are:
Let the two given sides be of measure \[3\: cm\] and \[ 6\: cm\] and the included angle between them be of measure \[30^{\circ}\].

Step 1: Join \[A\] to \[C\]. \[\triangle ABC\] is the required triangle.
Step 2: Draw a line segment \[m\overline{BC}=6\: cm\].
Step 3: With \[B\] as centre, draw an arc of radius \[3\: cm\] to cut \[\overrightarrow{BD}\] at \[A\].
Step 4: At point \[B\], draw \[m∠DBC=30^{\circ}\] using a compass.

Arrange the steps in the correct order.
skill: Construct a triangle given two sides and the included angle
feedback:
Recall that the correct order is as follows:
\[1)\] First, we draw a line segment, which is given by \[\overline{BC}\] \[\rightarrow\] step  \[2\]

\[2) \] Then at point \[B\], we construct an angle of \[30^{\circ}\] \[\rightarrow\] step  \[4\]

\[3)\] Then, with \[B\] as centre, we draw an arc of radius \[3\: cm\] to cut \[\overline{BD} \] and label the point of intersection as \[A\] \[\rightarrow\] step  \[3\]

\[4)\] Finally, we join\[\: \] \[A\] to \[C\], forming \[\triangle ABC\] which is the required triangle \[\rightarrow\] step  \[1\]

The steps to construct a triangle when two sides and the included angle are given are:

Step 1: Draw a line segment \[m\overline{BC}=6\: cm\].
Step 2: At point \[B\], draw \[m∠DBC=x \] using a compass.
Step 3: With \[ B\] as centre, draw an arc of radius \[4\: cm\] to cut \[\overrightarrow{BD}\] at \[A\].
Step 4: Join \[A \] to \[C\]. \[\triangle ABC\] is the required triangle.




Given that the red and blue arcs are centred at points \[B\] and \[E\] respectively and have the same radius, what is the angle \[x\]?
skill: Construct a triangle given two sides and the included angle
feedback:
Since the two arcs are of the same radius we have
\[m\overline{BE} =m\overline{BF} =m\overline{EF} \]

Thus \[\triangle BEF\] is an equilateral triangle and we have
\[x=m∠B=60^{\circ}\]

\[3\]) Finally, we label the point of intersection of \[\overrightarrow{AD}\] and \[\overrightarrow{BE}\] as \[C\], forming \[\triangle ABC\] which is the required triangle \[\rightarrow\] step \[3\]

The diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other at right angles.

q: Which of the following formulas is used to calculate the variance of grouped data?
skill: Measure range, variance and standard deviation
feedback:
The formula to calculate the variance of grouped data is \[\frac{\sum{fX^2}}{\sum f}-\left(\frac{\sum{fX}}{\sum f}\right)^2\].

\[\sqrt{\frac{\sum{fX^2}}{\sum f}-\left(\frac{\sum{fX}}{\sum f}\right)^2}\] is used to calculate the standard deviation of grouped data.

subject: Mathematics

\[\frac{\sum{fX^2}}{\sum f}-\left(\frac{\sum{fX}}{\sum f}\right)^2\] is used to calculate the variance of grouped data.

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following  formulas is used to calculate the variance of ungrouped data?

feedback:
The formula to calculate the variance of ungrouped data is \[\frac{\sum{X^2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum{X}}{n}\right)^2\].

\[\sqrt{\frac{\sum{X^2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum{X}}{n}\right)^2}\] is used to calculate the standard deviation of ungrouped data.

\[\frac{\sum{X^2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum{X}}{n}\right)^2\] is used to calculate the variance of ungrouped data.

q: For the values \[8, 8, 9, 5, 7,\] and \[3\], the variance is _____.
skill: Measure range, variance and standard deviation
feedback:
\[X\] \[X^2\]
\[8\] \[64\]
\[8\] \[64\]
\[9\] \[81\]
\[5\] \[25\]
\[7\] \[49\]
\[3\] \[9\]
\[\small\sum{X} = 40\] \[\small\sum{X^2} = 292\]

\begin{align}\mbox{Variance } &= \frac{\sum{X^2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum{X}}{n}\right)^2 \\\\&= \frac{292}{6} - \left(\frac{40}{6}\right)^2 \\\\&= 48.67-(6.67)^2 \\\\&= 48.67-44.49 \\\\&= 4.18\end{align}

Therefore, the variance for the values \[8, 8, 9, 5, 7,\] and \[3\], is \[4.18\].

subject: Mathematics
q: For the values \[6, 6, 6, 6,\] and \[6\], the variance is _____.

feedback:
\[X\] \[X^2\]
\[6\] \[36\]
\[6\] \[36\]
\[6\] \[36\]
\[6\] \[36\]
\[6\] \[36\]
\[\small\sum{X} = 30\] \[\small\sum{X^2} = 180\]

\begin{align}\mbox{Variance for ungrouped data} &= \frac{\sum{X^2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum{X}}{n}\right)^2 \\\\&= \frac{180}{5} - \left(\frac{30}{5}\right)^2 \\\\&= 36-(6)^2 \\\\&= 36-36 \\\\&= 0\end{align}

Therefore, the variance for the values \[6, 6, 6, 6,\], and \[6\] is \[0\].

\begin{align} \mbox{Standard deviation for ungrouped data} &= \sqrt{\frac{\sum{X^2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum{X}}{n}\right)^2} \\\\ &= \sqrt{\frac{273}{7} - \left(\frac{41}{7}\right)^2} \\\\ &= \sqrt{39-(5.86)^2} \\\\ &= \sqrt{39-34.34} \\\\ &= \sqrt{4.66} \\\\ &= 2.16 \end{align}

Therefore, the standard deviation for the values \[3, 5, 3, 6, 8, 7,\] and \[9\], is \[2.16\].

subject: Mathematics
q:
Find the variance from the given information:

\[∑fX=500\], \[∑fX^2=13000\], and \[\bar{X}=25\].
skill: Measure range, variance and standard deviation
feedback:
Var\[(X)=\frac{∑fX^2}{∑f}-\left ( \frac{∑fX}{∑f} \right )^2\]

As \[\bar{X}=\frac{∑fX}{∑f}\]

Var\[(X)=\frac{∑fX^2}{∑f}-(\bar{X})^2 --- (1) \]

Since \[\bar{X}=\frac{∑fX}{∑f}\]

\[∑f=\frac{∑fX}{\bar{X}}=\frac{500}{25}=20 \]

Substituting \[∑f=20\] into equation \[(1)\], we have

Var\[(X)=\frac{∑fX^2}{∑f}-(\bar{X})^2 \]

Var\[(X)=\frac{13000}{20}-(25)^2\]

Var\[(X)=650-625\]
Var\[(X)=25\]

subject: Mathematics
q:
Find the standard deviation from the given information:
\[∑X=200\], \[∑X^2=5000\], and \[\tilde{X}=20\].
skill: Measure range, variance and standard deviation
feedback:
Standard deviation for ungrouped data is given as:

S.D\[(X)=\sqrt{\frac{∑X^2}{n}-\left(\frac{∑X}{n}\right)^2} =\sqrt{\frac{∑X^2}{n}-(\bar{X})^2} --- (1) \]

Since \[\bar{X}=\frac{∑X}{n}, n=\frac{∑X}{\bar{X}}=\frac{200}{20}=10 \]

Substituting \[n=10\] into equation \[(1)\], we have

S.D\[(X)=\sqrt{\frac{∑X^2}{n}-(\bar{X})^2 }\]

S.D\[(X)=\sqrt{\frac{5000}{10}-(20)^2} \]

S.D\[(X)=\sqrt{500-400}=\sqrt{100}=10 \]

\[2, 5, 6, 7, 8, 8, 8, 9, 10, 12\].
skill: Measure range, variance and standard deviation
feedback:
Let \[X\] be the variable in the question. Therefore,

\begin{align}\bar{X}&=\frac{2+5+6+7+8+8+8+9+10+12}{10}\\\\&=\frac{75}{10}\\\\&=7.5\end{align}

Since the variance of ungrouped data is given by Var\[(X)=\frac{∑(X-\bar{X})^2}{n}\], we calculate the values of \[(X - \bar X)^2 \] in the following table.



From the values in the table, we get

Var\[(X)=\frac{∑(X-\bar{X})^2}{n}\]

Var\[(X)=\frac{68.5}{10}=6.85 \]

q: The frequency distribution below summarises the length of employment of \[35\] workers in a factory. Determine the size of the class.
skill: Construct grouped frequency table
feedback:
The size of a class (denoted by \[h\]) can be calculated by the following formula:
\[h\] = upper class limit \[–\] lower class limit \[+1\]
For the class \[1---4\], the class size is:
\[h=4-1+1=4 \]
This is true for other classes as well because in a frequency distribution table, the class size throughout the classes is kept constant.

Alternatively, we can calculate the size of a class using the formula \[h=\frac{range}{k}\], where \[k\] is the number of classes. We obtain
\[h=\frac{20-1}{5}=3.8\]
Using the rule of approximation, this is equal to \[4\].

subject: Mathematics
q: The marks obtained by \[30\] students in a test are recorded and presented in the frequency distribution table below. Find the upper class boundaries.
skill: Construct grouped frequency table
feedback:
The upper class boundary of a class is the midpoint between the maximum value of the particular class and the minimum value of the next class.
As an example, the maximum value defined for the first class is \[39\] while the minimum value defined for the next class is \[40\]. Hence, the upper class boundary is the midpoint of these \[2\] values.

\[\frac{39+40}{2}=39.5\]
Repeating this for every class, the upper class boundaries are \[39.5, 49.5, 59.5, 69.5, 79.5,\] and \[89.5\].
Note that the class limits differ from the class boundaries.

subject: Mathematics

Note that the class limits differ from the class boundaries.

q: If the minimum value in a dataset is \[20\] and there are \[5\] classes altogether, each with an interval of size \[10\], then what is the maximum value of the dataset?
skill: Construct grouped frequency table
feedback:
Let \[X_{max}\] and \[X_{min}\] be the maximum and minimum values respectively, and denote their difference by

Range=\[X_{max}-X_{min} --- (1)\]

The number of classes (denoted by \[k\]) and the size of each class (denoted by \[h\]) are related via the following formula:

\[h=\frac{Range}{k} --- (2) \]

Substituting equation (1) into equation (2), we have:

\[h=\frac{X_{max}-X_{min}}{k}\]

\[hk=X_{max}-X_{min} \]
\[X_{max}=X_{min}+hk --- (3)\]

Substituting \[X_{min}=20\], \[h=10\], and \[k=5\] into equation (3), we have:
\[X_{max}=20+(10)(5)=70\]

q: The salaries of \[30\] engineers in a company are shown in the histogram below. How many engineers had a salary of less than \[$3{,}950\]?
skill: Construct histograms with equal and unequal class intervals
feedback:
This is a histogram with unequal class intervals, so we need to convert the proportional heights (denote it by \[h\]) into frequency (denote it by \[f\]).
We know that \[f=h×(\mbox{Upper class limit}-\mbox{Lower class limit})\]

Therefore, for the \[1950-2950\] interval, we have
\[f=h×(2950-1950)= 0.006 \times 1000=6 \]

For the \[2950-3450\] interval, we have
\[f=h×(3450-2950)=0.01×500=5 \]

For the \[3450-3950\] interval, we have
\[f=h×(3950-3450)=0.006×500=3 \]

Thus, the number of engineers with a salary that is less than \[$3950\] is \[6+5+3=14\].

subject: Mathematics
q: We can use _____ to show the probability of an event.
skill: Find the probability of single events
feedback:
The probability of an event can be presented by using decimals, fractions, or even percentages.
For example, the probability of flipping a coin and getting heads as an outcome can be written as \[\frac{1}{2}\], \[0.5\], or \[50 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the probability of getting a factor of \[6\] while rolling a six-sided die.
skill: Find the probability of single events
feedback:
The factors of \[6\] are \[1\], \[2\], \[3\], and \[6\]. Therefore

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 4\\ \mbox{Total number of possible outcomes} &=6\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of an event}&= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of rolling a factor of } 6&= \frac{4}{6} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{2}{3} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{200}{3}\\\\ &= 66.66 \% \end{align}

Therefore, the probability of getting a factor of \[6\] while rolling a six-sided die is \[66.66 \approx 66.7 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q: What is the probability of drawing a card with an even number at random from a deck of \[52\] playing cards?
skill: Find the probability of single events
feedback:
There are \[5\] even numbers in a deck of playing cards, \[2\], \[4\], \[6\], \[8\], and \[10\]. Furthermore, they have \[4\] cards each. Therefore

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 5 \times 4\\ &= 20\\ \mbox{Total number of possible outcomes} &=52\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of an event}&= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of drawing an even number card} &= \frac{20}{52} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{10}{26} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{1000}{26}\\\\ &= 38.46 \% \end{align}

Therefore, the probability of drawing an even number card from a deck of playing cards is \[38.46 \% \approx 38.5\%\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Sara bought \[4\] chocolates, \[5\] toffees, and \[3\] erasers. She put them all in a bag. Find the probability of NOT picking an eraser at random from Sara's bag.
skill: Find the probability of single events
feedback:
There are \[9\] items in the bag that are NOT an eraser, \[4\] chocolates and \[5\] toffees. So, we have

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 9\\ \mbox{Total number of possible outcomes} &=12\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of an event}&= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of picking a candy from the bag pack} &= \frac{9}{12} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{3}{4} \times 100\\\\ &=75 \% \end{align}

Therefore, the probability of picking a candy from Sara's bag pack is \[75 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the probability of NOT drawing a \[2\] from this set of cards.
skill: Find the probability of single events
feedback:
There are \[3\] cards other than the number \[2\] cards. So, we have

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 3\\ \mbox{Total number of possible outcomes} &=5\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of an event}&= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability NOT drawing a } 2&= \frac{3}{5} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{300}{5}\\\\ &=60 \% \end{align}

Therefore, the probability of NOT drawing a \[2\] from this deck is \[60 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the percentage probability of spinning this wheel and getting a multiple of \[5\] as an outcome.
skill: Find the probability of single events
feedback:
There are \[5\] numbers on this wheel that are multiples of \[5\], namely \[25\], \[30\], \[45\], \[75\], and \[100\].

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 5\\ \mbox{Total number of possible outcomes} &=8\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of an event}&= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of getting a multiple of } 5&= \frac{5}{8} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{500}{8}\\\\ &= \frac{250}{4}\\\\ &=62.5 \% \end{align}

Therefore, the probability of spinning this wheel and getting a multiple of \[5\] as an outcome is \[62.5 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q: To find the probability in percentage we _____.
options: divide the decimal value of probability by \[100\]
multiply the decimal value of probability by \[100\]
multiply the total number of possible outcomes by \[100\]
multiply the number of favourable outcomes by \[100\]
skill: Find the probability of single events
feedback:
To find the probability in percentage we multiply the decimal value of probability by \[100\]. So,

\begin{align} \mbox{Percentage probability of an event} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100 \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Probability of an event, \[P(E) =\] _____.
options: \[\frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}}\]
\[\frac{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}}{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}\]
\[\frac{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}}{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}} \times 100\]
skill: Find the probability of single events
feedback: \begin{align} \mbox{Probability of an event, } P(E)&= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}}\\\\ \mbox{Meanwhile, the percentage probability of an event} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100 \end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q:
Sara drew multiple cards from a deck of playing cards and got the following outcomes.



What is the probability that the next card she draws is NOT a number card?
skill: Define experimental probability and solve related word problems
feedback:
Sara drew \[5\] number cards from the deck in total, \[3\] tens and \[2\] nines. As she picked a total of \[12\] cards from the deck, we have

\begin{align} \mbox{Total number of trials} &= 12\\ \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 12-5 \\ &=7 \\\\ \mbox{Experimental probability in percentage} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of not picking a number card next} &= \frac{7}{12} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{700}{12}\\\\ &= \frac{350}{6}\\\\\ &= \frac{175}{3}\\\\\ &= 58.3 \% \end{align}

The probability of NOT picking a number card next is \[58.3 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Maryam picked \[5\] green, \[8\] red and \[11\] yellow marbles from a box at random. Given that the box has at least one marble of each color present in the box, what is the probability that the next marble he picks from the box is NOT a red one?
skill: Define experimental probability and solve related word problems
feedback:
Maryam picked \[5\] green and \[11\] yellow marbles from the box. This means that we have a total of \[16\] outcomes where she did not pick a red marble. So,

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 16 \\ \mbox{Total number of trials} &= 5+8+11\\ &=24\\\\ \mbox{Experimental probability in percentage} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of not picking a red marble next} &= \frac{16}{24} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{2}{3} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{200}{3}\\\\\ &= 66.66 \% \end{align}

The probability of NOT picking a red marble next is \[66.66 ≈ 66.7 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Jay picked \[3\] green, \[6\] red and \[9\] blue marbles from a box at random. Given that the box has at least one marble of each colour present in the box, what is the probability that the next marble he picks from the box is green?
skill: Define experimental probability and solve related word problems
feedback:
Jay picked \[3\] green marbles from the box. Therefore,

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 3 \\ \mbox{Total number of trials} &= 3+6+9\\ &=18\\\\ \mbox{Experimental probability in percentage} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of picking a green marble next} &= \frac{3}{18} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{300}{18}\\\\ &= \frac{150}{9}\\\\\ &= \frac{50}{3}\\\\ &= 16.66 \% \end{align}

The probability of picking a green marble next is \[16.66 ≈ 16.7 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q:
In a probability experiment, a six-sided die was rolled multiple times. The frequency of the outcomes are displayed in the table below.



Find the probability of rolling a number smaller than \[3\] in the next roll.
skill: Define experimental probability and solve related word problems
feedback:
From the table in the question, we can see that numbers \[1\] and \[2\] were rolled \[5\] and \[9\] times respectively.

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 5+9 \\ &= 14\\ \mbox{Total number of trials} &= 5+9+5+8+8+5\\ &=40\\\\ \mbox{Experimental probability in percentage} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of rolling a number smaller than } 3&= \frac{14}{40} \times 100\\\\ &= \frac{1400}{40}\\\\ &= \frac{140}{4}\\\\ &= \frac{70}{2}\\\\ &=35 \% \\ \end{align}

The probability of getting a number smaller than \[3\] in the next roll is \[35 \%\].

subject: Mathematics
q:
Sara spins this wheel of fortune \[4\] times and gets the following outcomes.



Find the probability of her getting a score that is an even number in the next trial.
skill: Define experimental probability and solve related word problems
feedback:
Sara had \[2\] trials where her score was an even number, \[50\] and \[90\]. So, we have

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 2 \\ \mbox{Total number of trials} &=4\\\\ \mbox{Experimental probability in percentage} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of the next score being an even number} &= \frac{2}{4} \times 100\\\\ &= 2 \times 25\\ &=50 \% \end{align}

The probability of Sara's next score being an even number is \[50 \%\].

q:
Lulu took the following \[5\] cards out of a deck of playing cards.



What is the probability that Lulu will draw a face card next?
skill: Define experimental probability and solve related word problems
feedback:
\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= \mbox{Number of face cards} = 2 \mbox{ }\mbox{ } \mbox{ } \mbox{ } \mbox{ } (\mbox{King and Queen})\\ \mbox{Total number of trials} &=5\\\\ \mbox{Experimental probability in percentage} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage probability of drawing a face card} &= \frac{2}{5} \times 100\\\\ &= 2 \times 20\\\\ &=40 \% \\ \end{align}

The probability of drawing a face card next is \[40 \%\].

q:
In a probability experiment, a six-sided die was rolled \[20\] times. The frequency of the outcomes are displayed in the table below.



Find the probability of rolling a number greater than \[3\] in the next roll.
skill: Define experimental probability and solve related word problems
feedback:
From the table in the question, we can see that numbers \[4\], \[5\], and \[6\] were rolled \[4\], \[1\], and \[4\] times respectively.

\begin{align} \mbox{Number of favourable outcomes} &= 4+1+4 \\ &= 9\\ \mbox{Total number of trials} &=20\\\\ \mbox{Experimental probability in percentage} &= \frac{\mbox{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\mbox{Total number of possible outcomes}} \times 100\\\\ \mbox{Percentage experimental probability of rolling a number greater than } 3&= \frac{9}{20} \times 100\\\\ &= 9 \times 5\\\\ &=45 \% \\ \end{align}

The probability of getting a number greater than \[3\] in the next roll is \[45 \%\].
